nativeRange.habitat: Damp shaded surfaces in subtropical and tropical Asian forests; grows on rocks, tree trunks and submerged or emersed wood in or near streams. Reaches noticeably larger frond dimensions than the related Fissidens fontanus (Phoenix Moss).

decorPreferences: driftwood (especially branchy pieces that showcase the fern-like fronds)
porous rock, lava rock, Ohko/dragon stone or seiryu
stainless or plastic mesh sheets for moss walls and carpets
tied with cotton thread / fishing line or attached with cyanoacrylate gel until rhizoids anchor
never buried in substrate — the base will rot if covered
gentle to moderate flow keeps debris off the feathery fronds

goodWith: tetras, rasboras and other small community fish

summary: Fissidens Nobilis is a slow-growing freshwater moss from subtropical and tropical Asia, recognised by its larger, fern-like fronds compared to the related Phoenix Moss. It attaches to driftwood or rock rather than rooting in substrate, thrives under low light without CO2 injection, and suits planted and shrimp tanks well.
careNotes: Fissidens nobilis is a freshwater moss (bryophyte) distinguished from the smaller, more commonly available Phoenix Moss [fw-plant-068] by its larger, more strongly fern-like fronds. Like all Fissidens mosses, it is an EPIPHYTE — a plant that grows anchored to surfaces rather than rooted in soil — and must be attached to a hard surface such as driftwood, porous rock (Ohko/dragon stone pairs particularly well aesthetically), lava rock, or plastic/stainless mesh. NEVER bury the base in substrate, where it will rot. Attach with cotton thread or fishing line, or secure with cyanoacrylate gel (superglue gel); the tiny root-like structures called rhizoids will anchor the moss over several weeks, after which the binding can be removed. LIGHTING is low to medium; the species tends to thrive in low-tech tanks and shaded areas without supplemental light, and intense lighting is not required. CO2 is not required, though injection is optional — CO2-supplemented tanks generally produce denser, more compact growth. SIZE is roughly 3–8 cm in height per frond, noticeably larger than Phoenix Moss (typically 2–5 cm), which is the main way to tell them apart in the trade. FERTILIZATION demand is low — the moss absorbs nutrients directly from the water column and does not require a nutrient-rich substrate; aggressive fertiliser dosing can damage the delicate fronds. FLOW: gentle to moderate flow keeps the fine fronds clean; very weak flow allows debris to accumulate in the fern-like leaf pockets, which should be compensated for with gentle turkey-basting during routine maintenance. SHRIMP AND FRY REFUGE: the dense, branching habit provides excellent microhabitat for dwarf-shrimp larvae and newly hatched fry.
breedingNotes: Vegetative propagation only under aquarium conditions: separate any healthy fragment from the mat (the trade product is often shipped on a thread mat that can be unwound into clumps) and re-attach it to a new piece of hardscape. Every viable fragment regrows. Trim with aquascaping scissors and remove loose fragments before they drift onto other plants. True sexual reproduction (sporophyte formation) requires emersed growth and is essentially never observed in fully submerged aquarium tanks.

Wikipedia's species selection does not specifically list F. nobilis, but the family/order applies uniformly across the genus.

propagation: fragmentation (any healthy frond regrows when re-attached)
trim with aquascaping scissors to shape and densify
